attrs.alt: Microsoft Entra Private Access zero-trust traffic flow

content.text: What is Microsoft Entra Private Access?

Microsoft Entra Private Access is the ZTNA (Zero Trust Network Access) component of Global Secure Access. It replaces traditional VPN for private application access by routing traffic through the Microsoft SSE edge and the Private Network Connector — a lightweight outbound-only agent installed in the private network. Access is granted per application segment, enforced by Conditional Access per request, with no implicit lateral-movement trust between apps.
The Private Network Connector makes only outbound HTTPS connections to the Microsoft SSE edge on port 443. No inbound firewall rules or public IP addresses are required in the private network.
Tunnels private app traffic from the device
Terminates client tunnel, verifies identity, routes to connector
Relays traffic from SSE edge to private resources
Windows Server in the private network / Azure VNet
Logical grouping of connectors for regional routing
Defines private IP/FQDN/port combinations accessible via GSA
Registers a private app for granular per-app CA policy targeting

Install the connector on a Windows Server VM with line-of-sight to private resources. Deploy at least two connectors per connector group for high availability — the SSE edge load-balances across available connectors.

# Install the Private Network Connector on a Windows Server VM in the private network
# Requirements: Windows Server 2016+, outbound HTTPS to *.msappproxy.net on port 443

# Download connector from Entra portal -> Global Secure Access -> Connect -> Connectors
# Run as local admin:
.\MicrosoftEntraPrivateNetworkConnectorInstaller.exe REGISTERCONNECTOR='false' /q

# After install, register the connector with the tenant (opens browser for admin consent)
.\MicrosoftEntraPrivateNetworkConnectorInstaller.exe REGISTERCONNECTOR='true' /q

# Verify connector service is running
Get-Service -Name 'MicrosoftEntraPrivateNetworkConnector' | Select-Object Name, Status

# Verify connector appears in Entra portal
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'NetworkAccess.Read.All'
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET `
  -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/networkAccess/connectivity/branches' |
  Select-Object -ExpandProperty value
Defining App Segments with Quick Access

Quick Access is the simplest way to publish private resources: group FQDNs, IP ranges, and ports into a single policy object. All destinations in Quick Access share the same Conditional Access policy targeting.
Per-App CA Policy with Enterprise Applications

For granular control — for example, requiring phishing-resistant MFA to reach SSH bastions but only standard MFA for internal web apps — register each private resource as an Enterprise Application and target it individually in CA policies.

Connector groups pin private resources to specific connector instances, reducing latency for geographically distributed private networks. EMEA users accessing EMEA apps route through EMEA connectors; APAC users route through APAC connectors.

# Create connector groups to scope connectors to specific private network segments
# Use case: EMEA connectors for EMEA apps, APAC connectors for APAC apps

Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Application.ReadWrite.All'

# Create a connector group
$group = @{ displayName = 'EMEA-Connectors' } | ConvertTo-Json
$created = Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST `
  -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/onPremisesPublishingProfiles/applicationProxy/connectorGroups' `
  -Body $group

# Move a connector to the group
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method PUT `
  -Uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/onPremisesPublishingProfiles/applicationProxy/connectors/<connector-id>/memberOf/`$ref" `
  -Body (@{ '@odata.id' = "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/onPremisesPublishingProfiles/applicationProxy/connectorGroups/$($created.id)" } | ConvertTo-Json)

# List all connectors and their group assignments
Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET `
  -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/onPremisesPublishingProfiles/applicationProxy/connectors' |
  Select-Object -ExpandProperty value |
  Select-Object id, machineName, status, @{N='Group';E={$_.memberOf.displayName}}
Private Access traffic appears in the NetworkAccessTraffic table with TrafficType == 'private'. Monitor top destinations, failed access attempts, and connector health.

// Private Access traffic summary — top private app destinations
NetworkAccessTraffic
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where TrafficType == 'private'
| summarize Sessions = count(), Users = dcount(UserPrincipalName)
    by DestinationFQDN, DestinationPort, PolicyName
| order by Sessions desc
| take 30

// Failed private access attempts — users being denied
NetworkAccessTraffic
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where TrafficType == 'private'
| where PolicyAction == 'block'
| extend UserUPN = tostring(UserPrincipalName)
| summarize Count = count(), Destinations = make_set(DestinationFQDN, 10) by UserUPN
| order by Count desc
Connectors that have gone silent (no traffic in the last hour) may be offline or network-isolated. This query flags them for investigation.

// Connector health — detect connectors with no recent traffic (may be offline)
NetworkAccessTraffic
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1h)
| where TrafficType == 'private'
| summarize LastSeen = max(TimeGenerated), Sessions = count() by ConnectorId
| join kind=rightouter (
    NetworkAccessTraffic
    | where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
    | summarize by ConnectorId
) on ConnectorId
| extend Status = iff(LastSeen > ago(1h), 'Active', 'Silent')
| project ConnectorId, Status, LastSeen, Sessions
| order by Status asc
Entra user + device per access request
Automatic — only private app traffic tunnelled
Entra user + device per access request
Automatic — only private app traffic tunnelled

Each connector group should have at least two active connectors to survive a VM reboot or network outage. The SSE edge round-robins across available connectors in a group. Verify HA posture with the following script.

# Verify connector high-availability — at least 2 active connectors per group
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'Application.Read.All'

Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method GET `
  -Uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/onPremisesPublishingProfiles/applicationProxy/connectorGroups?$expand=members' |
  Select-Object -ExpandProperty value | ForEach-Object {
      $group = $_
      $active = ($group.members | Where-Object { $_.status -eq 'active' }).Count
      Write-Host "Group: $($group.displayName) — $active active connector(s)"
      if ($active -lt 2) {
          Write-Warning "  RISK: fewer than 2 active connectors — no HA"
      }
  }

# Connector outbound network requirements (must be reachable on port 443):
# *.msappproxy.net, *.servicebus.windows.net, login.microsoftonline.com
# Test reachability:
Test-NetConnection -ComputerName 'login.microsoftonline.com' -Port 443
Test-NetConnection -ComputerName 'enterpriseregistration.windows.net' -Port 443

High P95 latency for private app sessions indicates a slow connector path — either the connector VM is under-resourced, the private network has high latency to the SSE edge, or the destination app itself is slow.

// Private Access session latency distribution — identify slow connector paths
NetworkAccessTraffic
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where TrafficType == 'private'
| where isnotempty(ResponseTime)
| summarize
    P50_ms = percentile(ResponseTime, 50),
    P95_ms = percentile(ResponseTime, 95),
    P99_ms = percentile(ResponseTime, 99),
    Sessions = count()
    by DestinationFQDN, ConnectorId
| order by P95_ms desc
| take 20
Troubleshooting a Failed Private Access Connection
When a user cannot reach a private resource, work through the following diagnostic steps in order — from the endpoint client, through the connector, to the app segment configuration.

# Troubleshoot Private Access connectivity for a specific user

# Step 1: Check if GSA Client is tunnelling on the endpoint
Get-NetAdapter | Where-Object { $_.InterfaceDescription -like '*Global Secure Access*' }

# Step 2: Check effective forwarding policy on the client
# Registry populated by the GSA client after policy sync
Get-ItemProperty 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Global Secure Access Client' |
  Select-Object PolicyVersion, LastSyncTime, TenantId

# Step 3: Verify the app segment covers the destination
# In Entra portal -> Global Secure Access -> Applications -> Quick Access -> App segments
# Confirm the FQDN/IP and port are listed

# Step 4: Check connector connectivity from the private network VM
# Event log on the connector VM:
Get-WinEvent -LogName 'Microsoft-AadApplicationProxy-Connector/Admin' -MaxEvents 50 |
  Where-Object { $_.LevelDisplayName -eq 'Error' } |
  Select-Object TimeCreated, Message

# Step 5: Check NetworkAccessTraffic for this user's recent sessions
# (Run in Log Analytics):
# NetworkAccessTraffic
# | where UserPrincipalName == 'alice@contoso.com'
# | where TimeGenerated > ago(1h)
# | project TimeGenerated, DestinationFQDN, DestinationPort, PolicyAction, PolicyName
Client not signed in or licence not assigned
Sign in to Windows with Entra account; verify P1 + Private Access licence
App segment not matched — connection refused
FQDN or port not in Quick Access / Enterprise App definition
Add the missing destination to the app segment in Entra portal
Connection reaches connector but times out
Private network firewall blocking outbound from connector to destination
Allow outbound TCP on the required port from connector VM to destination
Connector shows as inactive in portal
Connector VM stopped or outbound port 443 blocked to *.msappproxy.net
Restart connector service; check NSG/firewall outbound rules
MFA prompt loops or fails at access time
CA policy requires MFA but user's MFA methods are not registered
Ensure user is MFA-registered; check AuthenticationMethodsRegistrationCampaign
Microsoft Entra Private Access replaces traditional VPN with per-app zero-trust access: only traffic matching defined app segments is tunnelled, eliminating implicit lateral-movement trust.
The Private Network Connector is outbound-only (HTTPS on 443) — no inbound firewall rules or public IPs are needed in the private network.
Quick Access groups multiple private destinations under one policy; Enterprise App registrations enable per-app Conditional Access policies for granular MFA and compliance enforcement.
Connector groups provide regional routing — deploy connectors in each network region and group them so access to local resources stays local.
All Private Access traffic appears in the NetworkAccessTraffic Log Analytics table (TrafficType == 'private') alongside Internet Access traffic from the same table.
Compared to VPN, Private Access enforces MFA and device compliance per access request, not just at tunnel entry — significantly reducing lateral movement risk from a compromised device.
